Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Faith seeking understanding · 11th – 14th c.

Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Medieval Latin thought turns psychology into a dense debate about soul, intellect, will, individuation, and divine illumination. Alongside the scholastic masters stand visionary and mystical writers who test how thought, desire, and spiritual transformation belong together.

1033 – 1109

Anselm of Canterbury

Faith in search of understanding: the mind’s ascent to God through reason alone.

c. 1217 – 1274

Bonaventure

The Franciscan mind on the journey of the soul into God.

1225 – 1274

Thomas Aquinas

Soul as the substantial form of a human being: the great synthesis of Aristotle, Augustine, and Christian revelation.

c. 1217 – 1293

Henry of Ghent

A secular master on divine illumination, intellect, and the self.

1248 – 1298

Peter John Olivi

A Franciscan dissenter on soul, will, and the reflexivity of self-knowledge.

c. 1287 – 1347

William of Ockham

Nominalism applied to mind: intuitive cognition, concepts, and the razor.

c. 1290 – 1343

Walter Chatton

Ockham’s sharpest medieval critic on cognition, propositions, and the intellect.

c. 1200 – 1280

Albert the Great

Aristotelian psychology, abstraction, and the hierarchy of living powers.

c. 1280 – 1322

Peter Auriol

Peter Auriol’s thought on mind is not a minor annex to his theology.

c. 1300 – c. 1358

Jean Buridan

Medieval nominalism, cognition, and the mechanics of assent and will.

13th c.

Hadewijch

Mystical desire, interior transformation, and the soul’s union with love.

1343 – after 1416

Julian of Norwich

Divine love, inward vision, and the soul’s healing union with God.

d. 1310

Marguerite Porete

A systematic reconstruction from The Mirror of Simple Souls and multilingual scholarship

c. 1300 – 1358

Gregory of Rimini

Divine illumination, will, and late scholastic theories of cognition.

c. 1219 – c. 1292

Roger Bacon

Perception, signs, and experimental psychology in a Franciscan key.

c. 1320 – 1382

Nicole Oresme

Late scholastic psychology, intellect, and anti-Averroist hylomorphism.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
